title: 39th International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2023 — Pré-Écolier (Grades 1-2)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: How many circles are there in the figure?
questions.solution: Count all circles in the figure on PDF page 1. There are 8 circles total.
solution_steps: See PDF page 1 for the figure.
Count every circle carefully, including any that overlap or are inside other shapes.
Total count: 8 circles.

questions.question_text: The picture shows 5 cubes viewed from the front. What is the view from above?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1. Looking at the 5 cubes from above, the arrangement matches option B.
solution_steps: Identify the depth arrangement of the 5 cubes from the front view.
Visualize rotating the viewpoint 90° downward.
The top view shows the pattern in option B.

questions.question_text: Each bowl contains four numbered balls. In which bowl is the sum of all the numbers largest?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1. Add up the four numbers in each bowl. Bowl A has the largest sum.
solution_steps: Read the numbers on the four balls in each bowl from the figure.
Calculate the sum for each bowl.
Bowl A has the largest total.

questions.question_text: Mr. Beaver rearranges the pieces to make a kangaroo figure. Which piece is missing?
questions.solution: See PDF page 1. Compare the completed kangaroo figure with the pieces shown. The gap in the kangaroo matches option A.
solution_steps: Study the completed kangaroo figure and identify the missing section.
Compare each option piece to the empty space.
Option A fills the gap correctly.

questions.question_text: My boat has more than 1 circle. It also has 2 more triangles than squares. Which boat is mine?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2. Check each boat: must have more than 1 circle AND triangles = squares + 2. Only boat E satisfies both conditions.
solution_steps: For each boat, count circles, triangles, and squares.
Eliminate boats with 1 or fewer circles.
Among remaining boats, check which has exactly 2 more triangles than squares.
Boat E satisfies both conditions.

questions.question_text: This is my grandfather's birthday cake. A large candle stands for 10 years and a small one for 1 year. How old is my grandfather?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the cake. Count large candles (each = 10 years) and small candles (each = 1 year). The image shows 7 large and 6 small candles: 7×10 + 6×1 = 76.
solution_steps: Count large candles from the figure: 7 (each worth 10 years = 70 years total).
Count small candles: 6 (each worth 1 year = 6 years total).
Total age: 70 + 6 = 76.

questions.question_text: Pablo puts 10 toy cars on this racetrack. How many cars are in the tunnel?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the racetrack. Count the visible cars outside the tunnel, then subtract from 10. 4 cars are visible, so 10 − 4 = 6 cars are in the tunnel.
solution_steps: Count cars visible outside the tunnel from the figure: 4.
Total cars = 10.
Cars in tunnel = 10 − 4 = 6.

questions.question_text: Steven drives from X to Y. At each crossing, he stops before going straight ahead. In total, how many times does he stop at a crossing?
questions.solution: See PDF page 2 for the map. Trace Steven's route from X to Y and count every crossing where he goes straight through. There are 14 such crossings.
solution_steps: Trace the route from X to Y on the map.
At each crossing, check if Steven goes straight ahead.
Count each such crossing: total = 14.

questions.question_text: There are 5 trees in a park. A beaver can see only two of the trees because all the others are hidden behind other trees. At which of the marked points is the beaver standing?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the park layout. From point D, three trees are hidden behind other trees in the line of sight, leaving exactly 2 trees visible.
solution_steps: For each marked point, draw sight lines to all 5 trees.
Check which trees are blocked by others along each sight line.
At point D, exactly 3 trees are hidden and 2 are visible.

questions.question_text: There are 24 squares in the picture. Suchit has coloured some of the squares. How many more squares need to be coloured so that half of the squares are coloured?
questions.solution: Half of 24 is 12. See PDF page 3 to count coloured squares: 9 are already coloured. So 12 − 9 = 3 more squares need to be coloured.
solution_steps: Half of 24 squares = 12 squares need to be coloured.
Count already coloured squares from the figure: 9.
Additional needed: 12 − 9 = 3.

questions.question_text: The two tokens with the question mark have the same number. What is each missing number so that the sum is 18?
questions.solution: See PDF page 3 for the token values. The visible tokens sum to 12. The two unknown tokens must sum to 18 − 12 = 6, so each equals 6 ÷ 2 = 3.
solution_steps: Read the values of the known tokens from the figure; they sum to 12.
Two unknown tokens (equal) must make the total 18.
Each unknown = (18 − 12) ÷ 2 = 3.

questions.question_text: Raha wants to finish the bee on the left according to the model on the right. Raha needs to win points to unlock parts of the bee. How many points does she need to win to complete the bee?
questions.solution: See PDF page 4 for the bee model. Add up the point costs for all missing parts of the bee on the left. The total is 13 points.
solution_steps: Compare the incomplete bee (left) with the complete model (right).
Identify which parts are missing from the left bee.
Sum the point costs shown for those missing parts: total = 13.

questions.question_text: The table has 30 boxes. After painting the boxes in row 3, row 6, column C and column D, how many boxes will be not painted?
questions.solution: The table is 6 rows × 5 columns = 30 boxes. Row 3 has 5 boxes, row 6 has 5 boxes, column C has 6 boxes, column D has 6 boxes. Subtract 4 overlapping intersections: (3,C), (3,D), (6,C), (6,D). Painted = 5+5+6+6−4 = 18. Not painted = 30−18 = 12.
solution_steps: Row 3: 5 boxes painted. Row 6: 5 boxes painted.
Column C: 6 boxes painted. Column D: 6 boxes painted.
Intersections counted twice: (3,C), (3,D), (6,C), (6,D) = 4 boxes.
Total painted = 5+5+6+6−4 = 18.
Not painted = 30−18 = 12.

questions.question_text: A sheet of paper is folded in half. Square and round holes are punched. How does the sheet look after it is unfolded again?
questions.solution: See PDF pages 4–5. When the folded paper is unfolded, each punched hole is mirrored across the fold line. The resulting pattern of holes matches option B.
solution_steps: Identify the fold line and which holes are punched on the folded sheet.
Mirror each hole across the fold line to find its symmetric counterpart.
The unfolded pattern matches option B.

questions.question_text: A student made the shape shown using 12 cubes. He put one drop of glue between any two cubes that share a common face. How many drops of glue did he use?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5 for the 3D shape. Count every pair of cubes that share a face (one glue drop per shared face). There are 11 shared faces in the arrangement.
solution_steps: Examine the 3D arrangement of 12 cubes from the figure.
Count each internal face where two cubes touch (one drop per touch).
Total shared faces = 11.

questions.question_text: Max wants to complete the puzzle shown. He has 5 different pieces. Which pieces does he have to use to complete the puzzle?
questions.solution: See PDF page 5. Fit the pieces from each option into the empty space of the puzzle. Only the set in option A fills the puzzle completely without gaps or overlaps.
solution_steps: Examine the puzzle shape and the 5 available pieces.
Try each set of pieces to see which combination fills the puzzle exactly.
Option A is the correct combination.

questions.question_text: Elvis has 6 identical triangles. Which of the following pictures can he make?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the triangle shape and options. Check each option to see if it can be tiled exactly by 6 copies of the given triangle. Only option A works.
solution_steps: Note the shape of the given triangle from the figure.
For each option, check if the total area equals 6 triangles and if the shape can be tiled without gaps.
Option A can be assembled from exactly 6 of the given triangles.

questions.question_text: Five children share a birthday and each child has their own cake. Lea is two years older than Jose, but one year younger than Ali. Vittorio is the youngest. Which is Sarah's cake?
questions.solution: Age order: Vittorio < Jose < Lea (= Jose+2) < Ali (= Jose+3). Sarah is the fifth child. After assigning ages to the other four from the cakes shown, the remaining cake belongs to Sarah and matches option C.
solution_steps: Age relationships: Lea = Jose+2, Ali = Lea+1 = Jose+3, Vittorio is youngest.
Order from youngest to oldest: Vittorio, Jose, Lea, Ali.
Sarah is the fifth child; her age is determined by the remaining cake.
Sarah's cake matches option C.

questions.question_text: The map shows five villages A, B, C, D and E, and the distances in kilometres between them. Only two villages are the same distance apart no matter which route you choose. Which are these two villages?
questions.solution: See PDF page 6 for the village map with distances. Calculate all possible route distances between each village pair. Only between B and E are all routes equal in total distance.
solution_steps: Read all distances between adjacent villages from the map.
For each village pair, find all possible routes and sum their distances.
Only B and E have the same total distance regardless of which route is chosen.

questions.question_text: Sam walks through a two-storey maze from the entrance to the exit, both located at floor 1. In what order will she find the wall stickers?
questions.solution: See PDF page 7 for the maze. Trace Sam's path through both floors from entrance to exit, noting the order in which she encounters each sticker. The sequence matches option B.
solution_steps: Trace the only possible path through the maze from entrance to exit.
Note the sticker encountered at each point along the path.
The order of stickers matches option B.

questions.question_text: Emma finished third in a solo dance competition. There were three dancers between her and last place. In total, how many dancers took part in the competition?
questions.solution: Emma is in 3rd place. There are 3 dancers between her and last place (positions 4th, 5th, 6th). Last place is therefore position 3+3+1 = 7th. Total dancers = 7.
solution_steps: Emma's position: 3rd.
3 dancers are between Emma and last place (positions 4, 5, 6).
Last place position = 3 + 3 + 1 = 7.
Total dancers = 7.

questions.question_text: Malik places one of the five pieces on the grid. He cannot rotate or flip the pieces. Which piece should he use to cover the numbers with the largest sum?
questions.solution: The grid values are 1,6,7 / 9,5,4 / 2,8,3. For each piece option, calculate the sum of grid numbers it covers (without rotating or flipping). The piece in option E covers the cells with the highest total sum.
solution_steps: Grid values: row 1: 1,6,7; row 2: 9,5,4; row 3: 2,8,3.
For each piece option, determine which grid cells it would cover.
Calculate the sum for each placement.
Option E covers cells with the largest sum.

questions.question_text: In total, Maria has 19 apples in 3 bags. From each bag, she takes out the same number of apples. Now the bags have 3, 4 and 6 apples. How many apples did Maria take out of each bag?
questions.solution: After taking apples, the bags hold 3+4+6 = 13 apples. She started with 19, so 19−13 = 6 apples were taken in total from 3 bags. She took 6÷3 = 2 apples from each bag.
solution_steps: Remaining apples: 3+4+6 = 13.
Total taken: 19−13 = 6 apples.
Taken from each bag equally: 6÷3 = 2.

questions.question_text: Three frogs live in a pond. Each night, one of the frogs sings a song to the other two. After 9 nights, one of the frogs had sung 2 times. Another frog had listened to 5 songs. How many songs had the third frog listened to?
questions.solution: Frog A sang 2 nights, so listened on 9−2 = 7 nights. Frog B listened 5 nights, so sang 9−5 = 4 nights. Frog C sang 9−2−4 = 3 nights, so listened 9−3 = 6 nights.
solution_steps: Total nights = 9; one frog sings each night.
Frog A sang 2 nights → listened 9−2 = 7 nights.
Frog B listened 5 nights → sang 9−5 = 4 nights.
Frog C sang 9−2−4 = 3 nights → listened 9−3 = 6 nights.
